---
status: public
shortDescription: "A variable is needed to instantiate an object"
shortCorrection: "Objects have no name and can exist without a variable referring to them"
concepts:
  - Allocation
  - Constructor
  - Call 
  - Expression
  - Name
related:
  - id: ObjectLabeledWithVariableName
    relation: Possible consequence
  - id: CannotChainMemberToConstructor
    relation: Possible consequence
  - id: NoAtomicExpression
    relation: Object instantiation not seen as expression
  - id: VariablesHoldObjects
    relation: Implied by
  - id: VariablesHoldExpressions
    relation: Maybe

    id: ObjectsMustBeNamed

  - id: jls13

One cannot write `new Object()` without assigning the object to a variable
(like in `Object o = new Object()`) and thereby giving it a name.

childMdx.rawBody:
Writing `new Object()` on its own is perfectly fine.
It will allocate a new object and invoke its constructor.
If the expression stands on its own
(i.e., as a separate statement, like in `new Object();`),
then the resulting value, the reference to the allocated object, is thrown away.

Given that the constructor may have had a side effect, doing this can still be useful.

### Reasoning by Analogy

#### Primitive Values Don't Need To Be Named

Assume someone wrote the following code to print the negation of 5:

```java
int a = 5;
System.out.println(-a);
```

If their explanation was:

> To work with the value `5` I first have to give it a name.
> Only then can I negate it.

You would say:

> Not really! You can directly negate the value,
> without first assigning it to a variable.

```java
System.out.println(-5);
```

#### And Objects Don't Need To Be Named

The same reasoning that applies to values of type `int`
also applies to values of _any other type_,
including instances of classes.
For example, here we allocate an Object of type `Person`,
and then we invoke method `getName()` on it.

```java
Person p = new Person("Kim"); // ObjectsMustBeNamed
System.out.println(p.getName());
```

Note that `new Person("Kim")` is a value of type `Person`.

The above code can be refactored to:

```java
System.out.println(new Person("Kim").getName());
```

### Overgeneralization This misconception may occur due to inappropriate generalization, if all examples of object allocations were embedded in initializations or assignments: ```java // declare a variable p and initialize it Point p = new Point(); ``` ```java // declare a variable p Point p; // assign a value to the variable p p = new Point(); ``` Students might not be able to break down those statements into their constituent parts: an assignment with a variable on the left-hand side and an expression on the right-hand side. Related to this, they might believe that there are [NoAtomicExpression](/misconceptions/Java/NoAtomicExpression/), i.e., they might not understand that object allocations like `new Point()` are expressions, and thus can be used within larger expressions. ### IDE If students use BlueJ as their IDE, this may have triggered this misconception.

In BlueJ, objects are shown as red rounded rectangles. All objects are labeled not just with their class (`Point`), but with a *name* and the class (`point1: Point`). Moreover, when a student interactively invokes a constructor, BlueJ prompts them for the "Name of the instance". ### Textbook The "Objects First with BlueJ" textbook includes UML-inspired diagrams where, similar to the BlueJ IDE, objects don't just have a type, but also a name (notation: "`myDisplay: ClockDisplay`").

Students may label an object in a heap diagram not just with the class,
but also with a name (of some variable referring to that object).

Students may not accept `new Object()` as a legal expression.

Students exhibit [CannotChainMemberToConstructor](/misconceptions/Java/CannotChainMemberToConstructor/).

Students say "allocate variable" instead of "allocate object".
